Unit 9: Verb Groups B1 & B2 (The Accented Verbs)

In Unit 4, you learned Type A verbs (like κάνω), where the accent is on the stem. Now we meet Type B—verbs where the accent is on the very last letter (-ώ).

These verbs are the "singers" of Greek; they have a distinct rhythm and are split into two "vowel teams".

Team B1: The "Alpha" Lovers (-άω)

These are the most common Type B verbs. They love the letter α. Examples: αγαπώ (αγαπάω), μιλάω, ρωτάω, ξυπνάω**.

Pronoun Ending Example: μιλάω (speak)
(Εγώ) -άω μιλάω
(Εσύ) -άς μιλάς
(Αυτός/ή/ό) -άει μιλάει
(Εμείς) -άμε μιλάμε
(Εσείς) -άτε μιλάτε
(Αυτοί/ές/ά) -άνε μιλάνε

[!TIP] Spoken vs. Written
You might see αγαπώ (Formal) and αγαπάω (Spoken). They are the same! For a natural Masterclass feel, we recommend the -άω pattern as it’s the most common in modern Greece.

Team B2: The "Oo" Lovers (-ώ)

These verbs are less common but very important. They prefer the -ούμε sound in the plural. Examples: μπορώ, ζω, τηλεφωνώ, οδηγώ**.

Pronoun Ending Example: μπορώ (can)
(Εγώ) -ώ μπορώ
(Εσύ) -είς μπορείς
(Αυτός/ή/ό) -εί μπορεί
(Εμείς) -ούμε μπορούμε
(Εσείς) -είτε μπορείτε
(Αυτοί/ές/ά) -ούν μπορούν

How to tell them apart?

  • If a verb has an -άω version (like μιλάω), it's B1.
  • If it ONLY ends in -ώ (like μπορώ), it's B2.

🎯 Unit 9 Mastery Checklist

  • [ ] I can conjugate μιλάω (B1) and μπορώ (B2) in the present tense.
  • [ ] I know that B1 verbs use -άμε and -άτε for 'we' and 'you all'.
  • [ ] I recognize that B2 endings look like Type A endings but with an extra vowel "kick."

Up Next: Unit 10: The Subjunctive Mood (Intro to 'να')
